title: Addiction and PTSD
subtitle: How do addiction and PTSD overlap?
author: Caroline

seo.title: Addiction and PTSD | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Addiction and PTSD. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
PTSD can develop from many different kinds of trauma, including witnessing violence on a battlefield.

What is PTSD?

Many people experience a traumatic event at some point during their life. However, not everyone who experiences trauma develops PTSD.

A person with PTSD has experienced an event in which they were exposed to:

  • Death
  • Threatened death
  • Injury
  • Threatened injury
  • Sexual violence
  • Threatened sexual violence

Exposure to trauma can happen in many ways. A person with PTSD may have:

  • Experienced the traumatic event directly.
  • Witnessed the traumatic event happen to another person or group of people.
  • Indirectly experienced the traumatic event by being told about it in detail or by seeing its after effects
  • Learned that the event occurred to a loved one.

Living through a natural disaster or a fire could result in PTSD.

A person with PTSD has 3 primary symptom clusters:

Reexperiencing: They re-experience the trauma through painful memories and physiological symptoms. This can include intrusive thoughts and flashbacks of the trauma.

Avoidance: They try to avoid re-experiencing memories of the trauma. They also try to avoid negative emotions that might trigger a survival response. This can include avoiding persons and places that remind them of the trauma and attempting to suppress distressing thoughts.

Hyperarousal: They live as though they’re in a high degree of danger, even when they’re physically safe. This can include constantly scanning the environment for threats and being easily startled.

PTSD and substance use

According to the National Comorbidity Study (NCS),  people with PTSD are:

  • 1.6 times more likely to develop a nicotine use disorder
  • 3.2 times more likely to develop an alcohol use disorder
  • 3.9 times more likely to develop an illicit substance use disorder

(When compared to people who do not have PTSD.)

The commonalities between PTSD and substance use

PTSD and substance use disorders have a lot in common. Both addiction and PTSD create survival drives that are triggered by associations in the environment. Both of these survival drives elicit avoidance, for different reasons.

Persons with PTSD try to avoid things that trigger re-experiencing of symptoms -- this is part of PTSD’s symptomology. Places that remind individuals with PTSD of the trauma that they experienced feel dangerous. Sometimes when they are in these places, they feel extreme physical discomfort. When they avoid these places, their intense discomfort and anxiety dissipate. This leads to the avoidant behavior being reinforced, increasing the likelihood that it will occur again and again.

Panic attacks are a common symptom of PTSD. People who experience panic attacks report that they feel like they're "drowning on dry land."

Persons with addictions also have strong associations with their environment. Many places can become associated with substance use. Those places can elicit strong cravings to use, even years into sobriety.

Thus, individuals who are in recovery often avoid places that provide access to their substance of choice. This is not to avoid anxiety but instead is to avoid relapse.

Unlike much of the avoidance exhibited by persons with PTSD, this is an adaptive form of avoidance that can support recovery. Avoiding problematic places such as bars typically does not result in a high degree of functional impairment. It tends to support recovery, rather than hinder it.

The overlap of PTSD and substance use

Substances are often misused to regulate the symptoms of PTSD. A person may use substances to:

  • Decrease their hyperarousal. This can mean using a substance to sleep at night, or to get through a loud social situation.
  • Avoid unwanted emotions. This can mean using a substance to numb the distress of a flashback, or to avoid feelings of guilt.

When a person with PTSD tries to treat their hyperarousal and environmental reactivity purely through substance use, they may become emotionally and physically dependent on that substance. They may believe that they cannot regulate their emotions without that substance. They also may experience physical withdrawal symptoms when they do not use the substance.

The symptoms of PTSD can feel quite frightening and overwhelming. Individuals with PTSD often use substances in an effort to self-medicate.

How are co-occurring PTSD and substance use disorder treated?

There’s some debate in the treatment world over if you should try to resolve the trauma before engaging in a substance use treatment. This is a call that’s made on a case-by-case basis.

When working with individuals who experience both PTSD and SUD, we use evidence-based forms of cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT). These include EMDR, exposure therapy, and cognitive processing therapy. These interventions help the person learn how to successfully cope with their traumatic experiences without utilizing substance use as a method of avoidance.
